Kayaking the lagoon offers intimate wildlife encounters in a pristine wetland backed by the Southern Alps. Guided and self-guided options available.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Okarito Lagoon, the largest unmodified wetland in New Zealand and one of its best-kept secrets. Your kayak will carry you silently across three thousand hectares of tidal lagoon, through channels lined with native bush and backed by the towering wall of the Southern Alps. This is the only breeding ground for the kotuku, the white heron — one of New Zealand's rarest and most sacred birds, with fewer than 200 remaining. Paddle quietly and you may see one standing motionless in the shallows, or watch a royal spoonbill sweep its beak through the water. The lagoon changes character with the tides, revealing mudflats teeming with wading birds at low water and becoming a glassy mirror at high tide. The tiny settlement of Okarito has just 30 permanent residents, and the sense of solitude here is profound.

reviews.summary: The Okarito Lagoon Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Kayak New Zealand's largest unmodified wetland, home to the rare kotuku white heron. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: West Coast Treetop Walkway
pois.short: Walk among the canopy of ancient rainforest on a steel treetop platform.
pois.commentary: The West Coast Treetop Walkway near Hokitika rises 20 metres into the canopy of a lowland podocarp rainforest on a steel and timber platform. The walkway spans 450 metres with a 47-metre-high tower offering panoramic views of the forest, Lake Mahinapua, and the Southern Alps.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the West Coast Treetop Walkway, where a network of steel platforms and walkways lifts you 20 metres into the canopy of one of New Zealand's finest lowland rainforests. The 450-metre walkway threads through rimu, kahikatea, and kamahi trees that are hundreds of years old, and at canopy level you'll see an entirely different world from the forest floor — epiphytes clinging to branches, birds flitting through the leaves, and shafts of light filtering through the green. The highlight is the Hokitika Tower, rising 47 metres above the forest floor to give you panoramic views across Lake Mahinapua to the snow-capped Southern Alps. Standing at the top with the forest stretching out in every direction below you, you really grasp the vastness of the West Coast bush. It's peaceful, beautiful, and gently exhilarating all at once.

reviews.summary: The West Coast Treetop Walkway adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Walk among the canopy of ancient rainforest on a steel treetop platform. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Underworld Adventures Charleston
pois.short: Float through glow-worm-lit caves on an underground river adventure.
pois.commentary: Underworld Adventures at Charleston offers guided cave experiences including the Rainforest to Underworld tour, where you travel through rainforest to reach an underground river and float through caves illuminated by thousands of glow-worms. The cave system includes ancient fossils and dramatic limestone formations.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Charleston, gateway to an extraordinary underground world. Your adventure begins with a journey through dense West Coast rainforest before you descend into a cave system that has been sculpted by water over millions of years. The highlight is floating down an underground river on a tube, drifting through darkness as thousands of glow-worms light up the cave ceiling above you like a living galaxy. These tiny creatures — actually the larvae of a native fungus gnat — produce a bioluminescent glow to attract prey, and in the darkness of the cave, the effect is truly magical. The cave system also contains ancient marine fossils embedded in the limestone walls, evidence that this land was once beneath the sea. It's an adventure that combines wonder, geology, and a healthy dose of getting wet in the dark.

reviews.summary: The Underworld Adventures Charleston adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Float through glow-worm-lit caves on an underground river adventure. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Skydive Franz Josef & Fox Glacier
pois.short: Skydive over glaciers, rainforest, and the Tasman Sea all in one jump.
pois.commentary: Skydive Franz Josef offers tandem jumps from up to 20,000 feet — the highest commercial skydive in New Zealand. Views span glaciers, the Southern Alps, ancient rainforest, and the Tasman Sea coastline. The unique geography means you can see mountains, forest, and ocean simultaneously during freefall.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at what might be the most scenic skydiving drop zone in the world. Skydive Franz Josef takes you up to 20,000 feet — higher than any other commercial skydive in New Zealand — and the views from up there are beyond belief. You'll see both the east and west coasts of the South Island, the white rivers of Franz Josef and Fox Glaciers flowing through emerald rainforest, and the infinite blue of the Tasman Sea. When you jump, you freefall for up to 75 seconds, hurtling toward a landscape that combines every type of New Zealand scenery in a single panorama — snow, ice, mountains, rainforest, rivers, and ocean. It's the combination of altitude, scenery, and sheer diversity of landscape that makes this jump truly unique. If you're going to skydive once in your life, this is a very strong contender.

reviews.summary: The Skydive Franz Josef & Fox Glacier adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Skydive over glaciers, rainforest, and the Tasman Sea all in one jump. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: West Coast Sand Dune Adventures
pois.short: Ride ATVs and sandboard down massive coastal dunes on the West Coast.
pois.commentary: The West Coast's coastal sand dunes near Hokitika offer quad biking and sandboarding experiences. The dunes rise up to 30 metres above the beach with views of the Tasman Sea. The remote, wild setting on the West Coast adds to the adventure atmosphere.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the West Coast's coastal sand dunes, where mountains of golden sand meet the wild Tasman Sea. These aren't gentle ripples — some of these dunes rise over 30 metres above the beach, and you're about to conquer them. Climb aboard an ATV and carve your way across the dune field, or grab a sandboard and slide down the steep faces at surprising speed. The West Coast setting is dramatic — to one side, the dunes and the crashing surf; to the other, dense native bush backed by the distant Southern Alps. The sand is firm enough for serious speed but soft enough for safe landings when sandboarding. This is a raw, unpolished adventure that captures the wild spirit of the West Coast — no fancy infrastructure, just sand, sea, and adrenaline. Bring sunscreen and a sense of adventure.

reviews.summary: The West Coast Sand Dune Adventures adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride ATVs and sandboard down massive coastal dunes on the West Coast. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Lake Brunner Trout Fishing
pois.short: Fish for trophy trout in the untouched waters of Lake Brunner.
pois.commentary: Lake Brunner (Moana Kotuku) is the West Coast's largest lake and one of New Zealand's top trout fisheries. The lake and its tributaries hold large brown and rainbow trout in pristine bush-fringed waters. Guided trips use fly fishing and trolling methods. The Arnold River outlet is particularly renowned.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Lake Brunner, known in Maori as Moana Kotuku — the Sea of the White Heron. This is the largest lake on the West Coast and one of New Zealand's most beautiful and productive trout fisheries. The lake is fringed by dense native bush, backed by the Paparoa Range, and home to brown and rainbow trout that grow large on the rich food supply. The Arnold River, which flows from the lake, is particularly renowned among fly fishers for its trophy-sized brown trout — fish over five pounds are not uncommon, and the sight of a large trout rising to a dry fly in the Arnold's clear water is enough to make any angler's heart race. Guided trips will put you on the fish, whether you prefer trolling the lake's deep waters or casting a fly in the river shallows. The West Coast bush and mountain scenery surrounding the lake is world-class, and on a calm morning, the reflections on the water are perfect.

reviews.summary: The Lake Brunner Trout Fishing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Fish for trophy trout in the untouched waters of Lake Brunner. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Monteith's Brewery
pois.short: West Coast brewing since 1868 — the Greymouth brewery bar serves heritage beers with hearty Coast fare.
pois.commentary: Monteith's has been brewing on the West Coast since 1868, making it one of New Zealand's oldest brewing sites. The Greymouth brewery bar serves the full range alongside hearty pub food — think whitebait, venison and blue cod alongside tasting trays of their beers. The Original Ale is a classic, and the seasonal Radler is refreshing after a Coast road trip. Heritage and good beer in equal measure.

reviews.summary: The heritage story adds depth to the beer experience. The food is hearty and suits the Coast. Some find it touristy, but the beer quality is consistent.

pois.name: Fat Pipi Pizza
pois.short: Hokitika's famous whitebait pizza — 140g of fresh whitebait on a wood-fired base. Only on the Coast.
pois.commentary: Fat Pipi has created what might be New Zealand's most unique pizza — a handmade base covered with garlic butter, mozzarella and 140 grams of fresh West Coast whitebait, finished with capers, Parmesan and lemon. It's only available during whitebait season (September-October), and it's extraordinary. Outside season, the standard pizza menu is excellent too. A quintessential West Coast food experience.

reviews.summary: The whitebait pizza is a must-try during season. Creative and delicious. Outside whitebait season, the regular pizzas are still very good.

pois.name: Ocean View Restaurant
pois.short: Hokitika beachfront restaurant — West Coast seafood and whitebait with Tasman Sea sunset views.
pois.commentary: The Ocean View sits right on Hokitika beach with front-row seats for the famous West Coast sunsets. The menu showcases Coast specialities — whitebait patties (in season), blue cod, venison and local greens. The sunset views across the Tasman Sea are spectacular, and the restaurant smartly times dinner service to coincide. A Hokitika must-visit, especially September-October for whitebait season.

reviews.summary: The sunset views and whitebait patties are highlights. A solid coastal dining experience. Some find the menu limited outside whitebait season.

pois.name: Buccleugh's on High
pois.short: Greymouth's favourite pub restaurant — seasonal whitebait, venison and blue cod in the Recreation Hotel.
pois.commentary: Buccleugh's at the Recreation Hotel is where Greymouth locals go for a proper dinner. The menu celebrates West Coast produce — whitebait (in season), venison from the Southern Alps foothills, fresh blue cod from the Tasman — in generous, unfussy preparations. The wine list is NZ-focused, and the atmosphere is warm and welcoming. It's Coast dining at its most authentic — no pretension, just great local food.

reviews.summary: Authentic West Coast dining that locals love. The whitebait and venison are highlights. Some find the pub setting basic, but the food quality shines.

pois.name: Hokitika Wildfoods Festival
pois.short: Annual March festival celebrating the Coast's wild food — huhu grubs, whitebait, possum and more.
pois.commentary: The Hokitika Wildfoods Festival is one of New Zealand's most distinctive food events. Held each March, it celebrates the West Coast's tradition of living off the land (and sea) with stalls selling huhu grubs, mountain oysters, whitebait, possum pies and other adventurous fare alongside more conventional food and craft beer. Live music and a carnival atmosphere make it a proper Coast party.

reviews.summary: A unique and entertaining food festival. The adventurous food is the draw. Not for the squeamish, but everyone has a great time regardless.

pois.name: Speight's Ale House Greymouth
pois.short: Greymouth's popular pub restaurant — Southern Man beers, hearty steaks and classic West Coast hospitality.
pois.commentary: Speight's Ale House in Greymouth is a reliable choice for a proper pub dinner on the West Coast. The beer selection covers the Speight's range alongside guest taps, and the food is hearty — think steak, fish and chips, and good old-fashioned pub fare. The atmosphere is friendly and welcoming, and it's where locals gather after work. Nothing fancy, just solid West Coast hospitality.

reviews.summary: Reliable pub food in a friendly atmosphere. The beer selection is decent. Good for a casual, hearty dinner on the Coast.

pois.name: Reefton Distilling Co
pois.short: Heritage gold-town gin distillery — small-batch spirits using native West Coast botanicals.
pois.commentary: Reefton Distilling Co has brought a new lease of life to this former gold-mining town by crafting premium gin and whiskey using native West Coast botanicals. The distillery tours take you through the process, from botanical sourcing (many gathered from the surrounding bush) to copper-pot distillation. The 1866 Gin — named for the year gold was discovered in Reefton — is their flagship. A great off-the-beaten-path discovery.

reviews.summary: The native botanical story is compelling. The gin quality is excellent. Reefton is off the main tourist trail, but that adds to the charm.

pois.name: Punakaiki Rocks Café & Bar
pois.short: Café beside the Pancake Rocks — West Coast food, craft beer and Tasman Sea views.
pois.commentary: Punakaiki Rocks Café sits right beside the famous Pancake Rocks and Blowholes, serving West Coast food with Tasman Sea views. The menu features whitebait (in season), blue cod, venison and hearty soups — exactly what you want after a coastal walk. The craft beer selection includes Monteiths and local brews. It's the obvious stop when visiting the Pancake Rocks, and the food quality is better than you'd expect from a tourist-adjacent café.

reviews.summary: Good food in a spectacular location. The whitebait fritters in season are excellent. Prices reflect the captive-audience location.

pois.name: Ōkārito — Rowi Kiwi Sanctuary
pois.short: Home of the rarest kiwi — the Ōkārito brown kiwi (rowi) with only 600 remaining.
pois.commentary: Ōkārito is the only home of the rowi, the rarest kiwi species with around 600 individuals. Okarito Kiwi Tours runs guided night walks with an extremely high kiwi sighting rate. The adjacent Ōkārito Lagoon is also excellent for white heron and royal spoonbill.

reviews.summary: Extraordinary kiwi experience. Sighting rate is very high. Book well ahead. The guides are exceptional.

pois.name: White Heron / Kōtuku Sanctuary
pois.short: NZ's only white heron breeding colony — guided jet boat and boardwalk to the nesting site.
pois.commentary: The white heron (kōtuku) breeding colony near Ōkārito is the only one in NZ, with about 30-50 pairs nesting each season. White Heron Sanctuary Tours runs guided visits by jet boat and boardwalk. The heron is highly sacred in Māori culture. Breeding season Oct-Feb.

reviews.summary: Unique NZ wildlife experience. The jet boat trip is fun. The herons are beautiful. Book ahead for breeding season.

pois.name: Punakaiki — Westland Petrel & Penguin Colony
pois.short: The only mainland breeding colony of Westland petrel, plus little blue penguins at the Pancake Rocks.
pois.commentary: The hills above Punakaiki host the world's only breeding colony of Westland petrel (tāiko). The rare seabirds return to their burrows at dusk from March to November. Little blue penguins also nest around the coast. The famous Pancake Rocks blowholes are a bonus.

reviews.summary: Unique seabird viewing. Petrel watching requires dusk timing and patience. The Pancake Rocks themselves are worth visiting.

pois.name: Hokitika Gorge — Freshwater Ecology
pois.short: Turquoise glacial river with native fish, longfin eels, and lush rainforest surroundings.
pois.commentary: Hokitika Gorge is famous for its turquoise water colour caused by glacial flour. The river supports longfin eels and native fish. The surrounding rainforest is lush and atmospheric, with good bush bird populations. A short walk from the car park to the swing bridge.

reviews.summary: Stunning water colour. Short easy walk. The bush birds are a bonus. Very photogenic.

pois.name: Franz Josef — West Coast Wildlife Centre
pois.short: Kiwi breeding facility in Franz Josef — see rowi and Haast tokoeka kiwi chicks being raised for release.
pois.commentary: The West Coast Wildlife Centre in Franz Josef runs a kiwi breeding programme for rowi and Haast tokoeka — two of NZ's rarest kiwi. Behind-the-scenes tours show the incubation and rearing process. The nocturnal kiwi house offers reliable viewing of adult birds.

reviews.summary: Excellent kiwi conservation centre. The behind-the-scenes tour is highly recommended. Good rainy-day activity.

pois.name: Lake Matheson — Forest Bird Walk
pois.short: Famous reflection lake surrounded by kahikatea forest — excellent bush birding on the loop track.
pois.commentary: Lake Matheson's loop track passes through mature kahikatea swamp forest rich in birdlife. Kereru, bellbird, fantail, and grey warbler are common. Dawn visits offer the famous Aoraki/Mt Cook reflection and peak bird activity. The forest is atmospheric and ancient.

reviews.summary: Beautiful walk at any time. Dawn is magical for both reflections and birding. The bush is spectacular.

pois.name: Haast Tokoeka — Jackson Bay Kiwi
pois.short: Wild Haast tokoeka kiwi in remote South Westland forests — one of the rarest kiwi populations.
pois.commentary: The Haast tokoeka is one of the rarest kiwi taxa, restricted to the forests of South Westland. DOC's recovery programme includes intensive stoat trapping and Operation Nest Egg. The Jackson Bay area is the most accessible part of their range, though sightings require night walks.

reviews.summary: Remote and challenging but meaningful. Contributing to kiwi conservation awareness. Night walks essential.

pois.name: Monro Beach — Fiordland Crested Penguin
pois.short: Walk through rainforest to a remote beach where Fiordland crested penguins come ashore July-November.
pois.commentary: Monro Beach south of Lake Moeraki is one of the most accessible sites to see Fiordland crested penguins (tawaki) on the mainland. The 40-minute walk through stunning rainforest leads to a wild beach where penguins come ashore during the breeding season (Jul-Nov). Late afternoon is best.

reviews.summary: Incredible penguin experience. The rainforest walk is beautiful. Timing and season are critical. Free.

pois.name: Cape Foulwind — Fur Seal Colony
pois.short: Large NZ fur seal colony on the walkway at Cape Foulwind — viewing platforms above the breeding rocks.
pois.commentary: The Cape Foulwind walkway near Westport passes above a large NZ fur seal colony. Viewing platforms provide excellent views of basking seals and, during breeding season, playful pups. The coastal walk itself is scenic, with views to the Paparoa Range.

reviews.summary: Reliable seal viewing. The viewing platforms are well positioned. Easy walk suitable for all ages. Free.

pois.name: Ross Goldfields — Kiwi & Bush Bird Walk
pois.short: Historic gold mining walks through regenerating bush — kiwi territory with excellent bush birding.
pois.commentary: The Ross Goldfields walking tracks pass through regenerating bush that is now kiwi territory. DOC pest control has allowed kiwi and bush birds to thrive. Bellbird, tui, kereru, and grey warbler are abundant. Historical gold mining relics along the track add interest.

reviews.summary: Nice combination of history and nature. The bush birds are excellent. Kiwi are nocturnal so dawn/dusk best.

pois.name: Ship Creek — Kahikatea Swamp & Dune Lake Walk
pois.short: Atmospheric walks through kahikatea swamp forest and coastal dune lakes — unique West Coast ecology.
pois.commentary: Ship Creek south of Haast offers two short walks showcasing unique West Coast ecology. The kahikatea swamp walk passes through atmospheric wetland forest, while the dune lake walk explores coastal lagoon ecology. Fernbird and grey warbler in the bush; wading birds on the lagoon.

reviews.summary: Atmospheric walks. The kahikatea swamp is otherworldly. Short walks but very rewarding. Free.

pois.name: Ōpārara Arches — Bush Bird Haven
pois.short: Ancient podocarp forest with great spotted kiwi, kaka, and rare cave spiders in limestone arches.
pois.commentary: The Ōpārara Basin in Kahurangi National Park has magnificent old-growth podocarp forest with excellent bush bird populations. Great spotted kiwi inhabit the forest floor, kaka are often heard overhead, and the massive limestone arches harbour rare cave spiders. Several short walks.

reviews.summary: Otherworldly forest. The arches are enormous. Great spotted kiwi territory. Allow time for all the short walks.

pois.name: Blue Pools — Freshwater Ecology
pois.short: Crystal-clear blue pools with giant trout visible in the depths — surrounded by ancient beech forest.
pois.commentary: The Blue Pools on the Makarora River are famous for their extraordinary clarity and blue-green colour. Giant rainbow and brown trout are visible in the pools, along with native fish. The short walk through beech forest to the swing bridge is magical. Bush birds are abundant.

reviews.summary: Stunning water clarity. The trout are enormous. Short easy walk. Very popular — arrive early.

pois.name: Jackson Bay — Fur Seals & Marine Life
pois.short: Remote South Westland bay with fur seals, Fiordland crested penguins, and Hector's dolphins.
pois.commentary: Jackson Bay at the end of the road in South Westland is one of NZ's most remote accessible coastal sites. NZ fur seals haul out on the rocks, Fiordland crested penguins nest nearby (Jul-Nov), and Hector's dolphins occasionally visit the bay. The Whitebaiting Museum is a quirky bonus.

reviews.summary: Remote end-of-the-road atmosphere. Good wildlife variety. The drive in is scenic. Allow a full day from Fox Glacier.

pois.name: Left Bank Art Gallery Greymouth
pois.short: Greymouth gallery showcasing West Coast artists, pounamu carving, and the creative spirit of the Coast.
pois.commentary: The Left Bank Art Gallery in Greymouth is one of the West Coast's principal gallery spaces, showcasing the work of local and regional artists in a heritage building in the centre of town. The gallery reflects the distinctive creative spirit of the West Coast — a region whose dramatic landscapes, isolation, and independent character produce art with a raw, powerful quality. Exhibitions rotate regularly and feature painting, sculpture, photography, pounamu (greenstone) carving, and mixed media. The West Coast has a strong tradition of pounamu carving, and the gallery often features work by local carvers alongside contemporary art. The gallery serves as a community cultural hub in a region where arts infrastructure is limited. Admission is free or by donation. The Left Bank precinct also houses other creative businesses and eateries. Greymouth is the largest town on the West Coast and serves as a base for exploring the region's natural attractions. The gallery provides a welcome cultural counterpoint to the outdoor adventures that dominate most West Coast itineraries.

reviews.summary: A nice community gallery with interesting local art that captures the spirit of the West Coast. The pounamu carving work is particularly beautiful. A good rainy-day option in Greymouth.

pois.name: Reefton Powerhouse Heritage Centre
pois.short: Heritage centre celebrating Reefton's pioneering role as one of the first electrified towns in the Southern Hemisphere.
pois.commentary: The Reefton Powerhouse Heritage Centre celebrates one of New Zealand's most remarkable industrial achievements — in 1888, the small West Coast gold mining town of Reefton became one of the first places in the southern hemisphere (and the first in New Zealand) to have a public electricity supply, powered by a small hydroelectric dynamo. The heritage centre, located in the historic powerhouse building, tells the story of this extraordinary achievement and the wider history of Reefton's gold mining era. Interactive displays explain the early electrical technology, and original equipment is on display. Reefton's claim to early electrification predates many major cities worldwide and reflects the innovative spirit of New Zealand's gold rush communities. The town itself retains wonderful character with heritage buildings along its main street and a strong community pride in its pioneering history. Reefton is also the gateway to the Victoria Forest Park and excellent mountain biking. The powerhouse centre provides fascinating context for a town that punches well above its weight in historical significance.

reviews.summary: Fascinating story of a tiny town that lit up before most major cities. The heritage centre brings it to life well. Reefton itself is a charming town with great character. Worth the detour.

pois.name: Blackball Historic Mining Town
pois.short: Historic coal mining town famous as the birthplace of the NZ Labour movement, with authentic West Coast character.
pois.commentary: Blackball is a small former coal mining town nestled in the Grey Valley foothills, famous as the birthplace of New Zealand's organised labour movement. The 1908 Blackball Miners' Strike was a pivotal event in New Zealand history, leading directly to the formation of the Federation of Labour and ultimately the Labour Party. The town retains powerful vestiges of its mining past — the heritage buildings, mine sites, and workers' cottages tell the story of a tough, resilient community. The formerly Blackball Hilton (renamed after a legal challenge from the hotel chain) is a legendary pub offering characterful accommodation and hearty meals. Blackball salami, produced locally, has become a gourmet delicacy. The town also serves as the starting point for the Croesus Track, a tramping route over the Paparoa Range to Barrytown on the coast. Blackball's authentic, uncommercialised character and significant history make it one of the most interesting small towns on the West Coast. A genuine piece of New Zealand's social history, still very much alive.

reviews.summary: A fascinating and authentic piece of NZ history — the labour movement story is powerful. The pub is a classic and the salami is delicious. Real West Coast character without any tourist gloss.

pois.name: Hokitika Gorge Turquoise Pools
pois.short: Jaw-dropping turquoise river gorge surrounded by native bush — one of NZ's most photographed natural wonders.
pois.commentary: Hokitika Gorge is one of the West Coast's most spectacular natural attractions — a deep, narrow gorge where the Hokitika River flows in an extraordinary vivid turquoise colour, framed by towering native bush and dramatic rock formations. The intense blue-green colour is caused by glacial flour (finely ground rock particles) suspended in the water, which scatters light to create the vivid hue. A short, well-formed bush walk (about 15 minutes each way) leads from the car park through beautiful podocarp forest to a swing bridge spanning the gorge, offering perfect viewing and photography angles. The colour is most intense on sunny days and can vary with rainfall — after heavy rain, the water may be milky. The gorge is located about 30 kilometres inland from Hokitika township via a scenic drive through farmland and bush. The walk is suitable for most fitness levels. This is one of those natural sights that genuinely lives up to its photographs. Visit on a sunny day for the best colour and arrive early to avoid tour bus crowds.

reviews.summary: The colour of the water is absolutely unbelievable — photos don't do it justice. The short bush walk and swing bridge make it easily accessible. Visit on a sunny day for the most vivid turquoise. Stunning.

pois.name: Punakaiki Cavern Walk
pois.short: Short rainforest walk to a limestone cavern with glowworms, complementing the famous Pancake Rocks nearby.
pois.commentary: The Punakaiki Cavern Walk is a short but rewarding native bush track near the famous Pancake Rocks, leading to a large limestone cavern featuring glowworms. The walk takes about 20 minutes return through lush coastal rainforest with impressive nikau palms, tree ferns, and broadleaf canopy. The cavern itself is a collapsed limestone cave — a large, open grotto that shelters a colony of glowworms visible on overcast days or in the evening. The walk is much less crowded than the neighbouring Pancake Rocks track, offering a peaceful forest experience. The track is well formed and suitable for most fitness levels. The cavern provides a taste of the extensive limestone cave systems that underlie the Paparoa landscape. Combine with the Pancake Rocks and Blowholes walk for a varied experience of this unique geological area. The Paparoa National Park visitor centre provides good context on the region's distinctive limestone geology. Free access, no time restrictions. The forest is beautiful even without the cavern destination.

reviews.summary: A lovely short walk through gorgeous rainforest to an interesting cave with glowworms. Much quieter than the nearby Pancake Rocks. A nice complement to the main attraction and the bush is beautiful.

pois.name: Eco Rafting Hokitika
pois.short: Scenic rafting through the Hokitika Gorge and river system on the West Coast's turquoise glacial waters.
pois.commentary: Eco Rafting offers a scenic rafting experience on the Hokitika River, famous for its extraordinary milky turquoise colour created by glacial flour, finely ground rock particles suspended in the water. The trip takes you through native bush-lined gorges where the water colour is at its most intense, past rocky outcrops and through gentle rapids suitable for families and first-time rafters. Unlike adrenaline-focused whitewater rafting, this experience emphasises the natural beauty of the West Coast river environment, with guides sharing knowledge of the ecology, geology, and gold-mining history of the area. The river's colour photographs spectacularly and the bush backdrop adds to the visual drama. No prior rafting experience is required and all equipment including wetsuits is provided. The trip is weather and river-level dependent, with calm conditions offering the most vivid water colour. Departures from Hokitika with transport provided.

reviews.summary: A beautiful and accessible rafting experience showcasing the West Coast's incredible water colour. More scenic than thrilling, which suits families and non-adrenaline-seekers. Guides are knowledgeable and personable.

pois.name: Monteiths Brewing Co Greymouth
pois.short: Historic West Coast brewery in Greymouth with guided tastings and the story of Coast brewing heritage.
pois.commentary: Monteiths has been part of the West Coast brewing story since 1868, making it one of New Zealand's oldest beer brands. The Greymouth brewery offers guided tasting experiences that tell the history of brewing on the Coast, from goldfields beginnings to modern craft production. The tasting session includes samples of the Monteiths range, from the flagship Original Ale to seasonal and limited-release beers, paired with local West Coast food including whitebait if in season. The brewery building has heritage character and the tour provides insights into the brewing process. While Monteiths is now owned by a major brewer, the Greymouth operation maintains a connection to its independent origins. The brewery is centrally located in Greymouth, easily visited between the TranzAlpine train arrival and other West Coast activities. A good rainy-day option, which on the West Coast is a practical consideration.

reviews.summary: An enjoyable brewery tour with a genuine heritage story. The tasting session is generous and the food pairings add value. A good option for a wet West Coast afternoon in Greymouth.

pois.name: Ross Goldfields Heritage
pois.short: Historic gold-mining town with heritage trail, water races, and the story of NZ's largest gold nugget.
pois.commentary: The small town of Ross, south of Hokitika, was the site of New Zealand's richest alluvial gold finds, including the famous Honourable Roddy nugget, the largest gold nugget ever found in New Zealand at 3.09 kilograms, discovered in 1909. The Ross Goldfields Heritage Centre tells the story of the gold rush that brought thousands of miners to the West Coast in the 1860s. A heritage walking trail leads through the old workings, past water races, mine tailings, and historic cottage sites, with interpretation panels bringing the gold-rush era to life. The trail takes about an hour and is mostly flat. A replica of the Honourable Roddy is displayed in the heritage centre. The town retains a frontier character with its wooden buildings and small-town atmosphere. Located on SH6 between Hokitika and Franz Josef, it makes a worthwhile stop on the glacier road.

reviews.summary: A well-presented gold-mining heritage site that brings the West Coast's gold rush history to life. The walking trail is interesting and the museum has good displays. Worth a stop between Hokitika and the glaciers.

pois.name: Franz Josef Glacier Valley Walk
pois.short: Walk through temperate rainforest to the terminal face viewing area of Franz Josef Glacier.
pois.commentary: The Franz Josef Glacier valley walk provides one of the world's most accessible glacier experiences, leading from lush temperate rainforest to within view of the terminal face of Ka Roimata o Hine Hukatere (Franz Josef Glacier). The glacier descends from the Southern Alps to just 240 metres above sea level, making it one of the most accessible glaciers in the world. The free DOC walking track follows the Waiho River valley through a landscape shaped by glacial advance and retreat, with interpretive panels explaining the geology. While the glacier has retreated significantly in recent decades, its immense scale remains impressive. Guided heli-hike tours land on the glacier by helicopter for an ice walking experience, accessing features like ice tunnels and seracs. The glacier township provides accommodation, dining, and other activities. The combination of temperate rainforest and glacier ice within the same landscape is found nowhere else on Earth outside New Zealand.

reviews.summary: A remarkable walk through contrasting landscapes from rainforest to glacial valley. The glacier itself has retreated but remains impressive. Heli-hike tours for walking on the ice are unforgettable.

pois.name: Pancake Rocks & Blowholes Punakaiki
pois.short: Layered limestone rock formations and dramatic ocean blowholes at Punakaiki on the West Coast.
pois.commentary: The Pancake Rocks at Dolomite Point, Punakaiki, are one of the West Coast's most iconic natural attractions. These heavily eroded limestone formations resemble stacks of pancakes, created over 30 million years through a process of alternating hard and soft limestone layers being differentially eroded by wind and rain. At high tide, ocean swells force through cavities beneath the rocks, erupting dramatically through blowholes in spectacular displays of spray and sound. The viewing loop walk takes about 20 minutes along well-maintained paths and boardwalks with interpretation panels. The blowholes are most impressive during high tide combined with a west or southwest swell, check conditions for the best display. Located at Punakaiki on SH6 between Greymouth and Westport, the rocks are a natural stopping point on any West Coast road trip. The adjacent Punakaiki Cavern offers a guided underground experience. A cafe and information centre provide facilities. Free to visit.

reviews.summary: A fascinating geological wonder that is even more impressive when the blowholes are active at high tide. The short walk is suitable for all ages. Time your visit with the tides for maximum impact.

pois.name: Okarito Lagoon Kayaking
pois.short: Kayak through NZ's largest unmodified wetland, home to the rare white heron and royal spoonbill colonies.
pois.commentary: Okarito Lagoon is New Zealand's largest unmodified wetland, a 3,240-hectare coastal lagoon backed by rainforest-clad mountains and the distant Southern Alps. The lagoon is home to over 76 bird species, including the rare kotuku (white heron), which breeds exclusively at the nearby Waitangiroto colony, and royal spoonbills, oystercatchers, and numerous wading birds. Guided and self-guided kayak tours explore the lagoon's tidal channels and mirror-calm backwaters, with the mountain reflections creating spectacular photography opportunities. The tiny settlement of Okarito (population around 30) retains a frontier character and was the setting for Keri Hulme's Booker Prize-winning novel The Bone People. The Okarito Trig walk provides panoramic views of the lagoon, coastline, and Southern Alps. Located between Hokitika and Franz Josef on SH6, Okarito is a peaceful detour from the glacier highway. Early morning paddles offer the best birdlife and calmest water conditions.

reviews.summary: A serene and beautiful kayaking experience in pristine wetland surroundings. The birdlife is exceptional and the mountain reflections are stunning. One of the West Coast's hidden gems.

pois.name: Ōpārara Basin Arches Walk
pois.short: Ancient limestone arches and caves in pristine Kahurangi bush near Karamea.
pois.commentary: The Ōpārara Basin near Karamea contains some of the most spectacular limestone formations in New Zealand, including the enormous Ōpārara Arch, which spans over forty metres and is the country's largest natural arch. The walking tracks through the basin pass through pristine old-growth podocarp forest draped in moss and ferns, creating an atmosphere that feels genuinely prehistoric. The Moria Gate Arch, named for its resemblance to a Tolkien creation, is equally impressive. The cave systems in the basin are home to rare species including the giant cave spider and the Powelliphanta snail. The tracks are well-formed but the remote location means visitor numbers remain low, preserving the area's wild character. The drive to the basin from Karamea takes about an hour on gravel road. Bring a torch for the caves.

reviews.summary: One of NZ's most impressive natural attractions, barely known to most visitors. The arches are awe-inspiring and the bush is primeval. Remote but absolutely worth it.

pois.name: Brunner Mine Heritage Walk
pois.short: Site of New Zealand's worst mining disaster — 65 men killed in 1896, remembered on a moving heritage walk along the Grey River.
pois.commentary: The Brunner Mine site on the Grey River preserves the memory of New Zealand's deadliest industrial accident. On 26 March 1896, an explosion ripped through the mine, killing all 65 men underground. The heritage walk follows the river past mine remnants, coke ovens, and the memorial to the miners who lost their lives. Interpretive panels tell individual stories of the men and the families left behind. The walk takes about an hour and passes through regenerating bush reclaiming the old mine workings. It is a sobering and powerful place — a reminder of the human cost of the coal industry that built the West Coast.

reviews.summary: Visitors find the Brunner Mine walk deeply moving and historically important. The interpretive panels are well done and bring the 1896 disaster to life. A quieter heritage site that rewards those who take the time.

pois.name: Coaltown Museum, Westport
pois.short: Westport's coal mining museum — walk through a replica mine and discover the industry that shaped the West Coast.
pois.commentary: Coaltown Museum in Westport tells the story of the Buller coalfields that drove the region's economy for over a century. The centrepiece is a walk-through replica coal mine complete with sound effects and low lighting that gives a visceral sense of working underground. Exhibits cover the geology of coal formation, the engineering of the Denniston Incline, and the social history of mining communities. The museum also houses displays on the Buller region's broader history including brewing and pioneer life. It is well-curated and genuinely informative — one of the better small regional museums on the West Coast.

reviews.summary: The replica mine walk-through is the standout exhibit and gives a real sense of underground conditions. Well-curated displays cover more than just mining. Good value for the entry price.

pois.name: Denniston Plateau
pois.short: A ghost coal mining settlement on a windswept plateau — once home to 1,500 people, served by the steepest rail incline in the world.
pois.commentary: Denniston Plateau sits 600 metres above the Waimangaroa Valley, connected to the world below by the Denniston Incline — once the steepest rail incline in the world, lowering coal wagons on steel cables at a terrifying gradient. At its peak in the early 1900s, the plateau was home to 1,500 people living in a tight-knit mining community battered by some of New Zealand's harshest weather. Today the settlement is largely reclaimed by bush and scrub, with foundations, mine entrances, and the incline machinery hinting at the life that once existed here. Walking tracks explore the old township and the incline itself. Jenny Pattrick's novel 'The Denniston Rose' brought this remote place to wider attention.

reviews.summary: The atmosphere of the abandoned plateau is haunting and memorable. Walking the incline track gives a real sense of the engineering achievement. Wind and weather add to the experience — dress warmly.

pois.name: Monteith's Brewing Co, Greymouth
pois.short: Greymouth's heritage brewery operating since 1868 — brewery tours, tastings, and a restaurant in the original brewhouse.
pois.commentary: Monteith's has been brewing in Greymouth since 1868, making it one of New Zealand's oldest continuous brewing operations. The brewery tour takes you through the process from grain to glass in a building that retains its industrial character. The tasting session at the end covers the core range plus seasonal and limited-release beers brewed only on site. The adjoining restaurant serves hearty West Coast fare designed to pair with the beers. While Monteith's is now owned by a major brewer, the Greymouth operation retains a genuine craft sensibility and the building itself is a piece of West Coast industrial heritage.

reviews.summary: Beer lovers enjoy the brewery tour and the chance to taste site-exclusive brews. The heritage building adds character. The restaurant is solid for a meal after the tour.

pois.name: Left Bank Art Gallery, Greymouth
pois.short: A free gallery in a grand former bank building — showcasing West Coast art, pounamu carving, and local craft.
pois.commentary: Left Bank Art Gallery occupies the impressive former Bank of New South Wales building in central Greymouth, its grand banking chamber now filled with art instead of ledgers. The gallery showcases West Coast artists working in painting, sculpture, photography, and pounamu (greenstone) carving. Pounamu features prominently given the West Coast's deep connection to the stone — Te Tai Poutini, the region's Maori name, references the taniwha guardian of pounamu. Entry is free, and the rotating exhibitions ensure there is always something new. The building alone is worth stepping into — high ceilings, original fittings, and the quiet grandeur of a colonial-era bank.

reviews.summary: Visitors appreciate the free entry and the quality of pounamu art on display. The heritage bank building is impressive. A pleasant stop in Greymouth that most travellers would otherwise drive through.

pois.name: Hokitika Glow Worm Dell
pois.short: A free glow worm experience just north of Hokitika — thousands of bioluminescent larvae lighting up a bush dell after dark.
pois.commentary: Hokitika Glow Worm Dell offers one of the easiest and most accessible glow worm experiences in New Zealand, and it is completely free. Located on the northern edge of Hokitika just off State Highway 6, the dell is a short walk from the car park into a sheltered bush gully where thousands of Arachnocampa luminosa larvae create their blue-green bioluminescent glow. The experience is best on dark, still nights — avoid using torches or phone screens once in the dell, as light disturbs the glow worms. Allow your eyes ten minutes to adjust and the display becomes increasingly spectacular. It is a magical five-minute detour that rivals paid glow worm attractions elsewhere in the country.

reviews.summary: Visitors are delighted by this free attraction, with many saying it rivals paid glow worm caves. Best on dark overcast nights. Turn off all lights and give your eyes time to adjust for the full effect.

pois.name: Oparara Arches, Karamea
pois.short: Massive limestone arches hidden in ancient forest near Karamea — including the 219-metre Oparara Arch, one of the largest in the Southern Hemisphere.
pois.commentary: The Oparara Basin near Karamea contains some of the most remarkable geological formations on the West Coast, hidden beneath a canopy of ancient podocarp forest. The Oparara Arch spans 219 metres over the Oparara River — one of the largest natural arches in the Southern Hemisphere. Nearby, the smaller Moria Gate Arch opens into a cave system where bones of the extinct moa and Haast's eagle have been found. The access road is 16 km of gravel from Karamea, and the remoteness is part of the experience. Walking tracks lead to both arches through forest dripping with mosses and ferns. The Oparara Basin feels genuinely primeval — a landscape unchanged for millions of years.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe the Oparara Basin as otherworldly and well worth the remote journey. The scale of the main arch is breathtaking. The ancient forest setting adds an almost prehistoric atmosphere.

pois.name: Hokitika Museum
pois.short: Gold rush and pounamu history housed in Hokitika's handsome Carnegie library building — the story of the West Coast in one collection.
pois.commentary: Hokitika Museum occupies the town's 1908 Carnegie library building, a fine Edwardian structure gifted by the American philanthropist's global library programme. Inside, the collection traces two defining threads of West Coast history: the gold rush that brought 30,000 people to Hokitika in the 1860s, and the centuries-old Maori pounamu trade that drew people to the region long before Europeans arrived. Displays include gold mining equipment, pounamu taonga, shipwreck artefacts from the treacherous river bar, and photographs of the town in its boomtown heyday. The museum is well-curated and provides essential context for understanding the West Coast. The Carnegie building itself is one of few surviving examples of its type in the South Island.

reviews.summary: Visitors praise the museum's comprehensive telling of the gold rush and pounamu stories. The Carnegie building is handsome. A good rainy-day option and provides valuable context for exploring Hokitika.

pois.name: Hokitika Jade Factory & Workshop
pois.short: Watch master carvers shape pounamu (greenstone) or carve your own — hands-on connection to the West Coast's most treasured stone.
pois.commentary: Hokitika is the pounamu capital of New Zealand, and the jade factories along Tancred Street offer visitors a chance to see the stone worked by skilled hands. Watch master carvers transform raw river boulders into polished pendants, mere, and hei tiki using diamond-tipped tools and patient grinding. Several workshops offer hands-on experiences where you can design and carve your own pounamu pendant under guidance — a meaningful souvenir with genuine cultural significance. Pounamu holds deep taonga status in Maori culture, and the carvers explain the traditions and meanings behind different shapes. The experience connects you to a tradition of working this stone that stretches back centuries on the West Coast.

reviews.summary: The hands-on carving experience is a highlight — visitors love creating their own pounamu pendant. Watching the master carvers work is mesmerising. Staff explain the cultural significance with genuine respect.

pois.name: Charming Creek Walkway
pois.short: A walk along an old coal railway line through a dramatic gorge — tunnels, bridges, and relics of the Ngakawau mining era.
pois.commentary: The Charming Creek Walkway follows the former Charming Creek coal railway through the Ngakawau Gorge near Westport, offering one of the most atmospheric walks on the West Coast. The track passes through a tunnel blasted from solid rock, crosses bridges over the creek, and winds past rusting coal bins, collapsed mine entrances, and old locomotive parts slowly being absorbed by the bush. The gorge is narrow and dramatic, with limestone bluffs rising above and nikau palms lining the track. The full walk is about five hours return from the Ngakawau end. The combination of industrial archaeology and wild West Coast bush makes this unlike any other walk in the region.

reviews.summary: Trampers rate Charming Creek as one of the West Coast's best walks. The combination of mining heritage and gorge scenery is unique. The tunnel section is atmospheric and the bush regeneration is impressive.

pois.name: West Coast Treetop Walkway
pois.short: Walk 20 metres above the forest floor through the rimu canopy, then climb a 47-metre tower for views to the Southern Alps and Tasman Sea.
pois.commentary: The West Coast Treetop Walkway south of Hokitika lifts you into the canopy of ancient rimu and kamahi rainforest on a steel platform walkway 20 metres above the ground. The engineering is unobtrusive — the walkway threads between the trees without damaging them, giving eye-level access to the epiphytes, mosses, and birdlife of the upper canopy. The centrepiece is a 47-metre observation tower, the tallest structure on the West Coast, with 360-degree views spanning the Southern Alps to the Tasman Sea. On clear days the panorama is extraordinary. The experience is accessible to most fitness levels, with the tower climb the only strenuous section. It offers a perspective on the West Coast rainforest that you simply cannot get from the ground.

reviews.summary: The tower views are spectacular on a clear day, stretching from mountains to sea. The canopy walkway gives a unique perspective on the rainforest. Family-friendly and well-maintained.

pois.name: Shantytown Heritage Park
pois.short: A recreated 1860s West Coast gold rush town — ride a steam train, pan for gold, and walk through 30 heritage buildings.
pois.commentary: Shantytown Heritage Park near Greymouth recreates a West Coast gold rush settlement from the 1860s, with over 30 buildings including a church, jail, hotel, school, and blacksmith shop. A vintage steam train runs through native bush on a short but scenic loop, and gold panning in the sluice gives everyone a chance to find colour in their pan. The recreations are well-researched and many buildings incorporate genuine heritage materials salvaged from actual West Coast settlements. The overall experience captures the rough-and-ready character of gold rush life — the isolation, the improvisation, and the optimism that drove thousands to this remote coast. It works well for families and as a rainy-day activity.

reviews.summary: Families particularly enjoy the steam train ride and gold panning. The recreated buildings give a tangible sense of gold rush life. Good for a rainy day, which is conveniently frequent on the West Coast.

pois.name: Ross Goldfields Heritage
pois.short: Where New Zealand's largest gold nugget was found — a quiet historic town with heritage walks through the old goldfield workings.
pois.commentary: Ross was the site of New Zealand's richest gold discoveries, and in 1909 yielded the Donoghues, the country's largest gold nugget at nearly 100 ounces. The heritage walk through town passes old water races, mine shafts, and tailings that reveal the scale of the gold extraction operation. Jones Flat, the main mining area, is now a peaceful depression surrounded by bush — it is hard to believe the frenzy that once consumed this ground. The Ross Heritage Centre tells the story with photographs, artefacts, and a replica of the famous nugget. Ross is a small, quiet town today, and the contrast between its sleepy present and explosive past is part of the experience.

reviews.summary: History enthusiasts appreciate the well-preserved mining heritage and the story of the famous nugget. The heritage walk is easy and informative. A quiet, authentic alternative to the more commercialised Shantytown.

pois.name: Lake Kaniere
pois.short: A bush-fringed lake inland from Hokitika — swimming, kayaking, and a kahikatea forest walk through one of the West Coast's finest lowland stands.
pois.commentary: Lake Kaniere sits 18 km inland from Hokitika, a large scenic lake fringed by dense native bush and backed by the foothills of the Southern Alps. The lake is popular for swimming, kayaking, and picnicking in summer, with several access points along the western shore. The Kahikatea Walk on the lake's edge is a particular highlight — a short loop through a magnificent stand of New Zealand's tallest native tree, with buttressed roots and emergent crowns rising above the surrounding canopy. The scenic reserve also offers longer walking options and the Dorothy Falls track. Lake Kaniere is less visited than Hokitika Gorge but equally beautiful — a place where the West Coast's green intensity is fully on display.

reviews.summary: The kahikatea forest walk is a standout — visitors love the ancient trees. The lake is peaceful and great for swimming in summer. Less crowded than other West Coast attractions.

pois.name: Pororari River Track, Punakaiki
pois.short: A limestone gorge walk through towering canyon walls at Punakaiki — the quieter companion to the Pancake Rocks.
pois.commentary: The Pororari River Track starts just north of Punakaiki and follows the river upstream through a dramatic limestone gorge in Paparoa National Park. Towering grey-white canyon walls rise on both sides, draped with ferns, rata, and nikau palms that cling to every ledge. The track is well-formed and mostly flat, crossing the river on swing bridges and winding through groves of giant rata. Most visitors to Punakaiki only see the Pancake Rocks, but the Pororari gorge is arguably the more impressive natural experience. Allow about 2.5 hours return to the turnaround point. The track also connects to the Inland Pack Track and the new Paparoa Track for those wanting a longer adventure.

reviews.summary: Walkers rate this as the hidden gem of Punakaiki — far fewer crowds than the Pancake Rocks with equally stunning scenery. The limestone gorge is dramatic. The track is well-maintained and mostly flat.

pois.name: Hokitika Wildfoods Festival
pois.short: Hokitika's legendary annual food festival — huhu grubs, whitebait, wild pork, and everything foraged, hunted, or gathered from the West Coast bush and sea.
pois.commentary: The Hokitika Wildfoods Festival has been running since 1990, drawing thousands to the West Coast each March for a celebration of wild and foraged food. Stalls serve everything from delicate whitebait patties and wild venison to the more adventurous offerings — huhu grubs, mountain oysters, and wasp larvae. Live music, craft beer, and West Coast hospitality create a party atmosphere in the Hokitika town centre. The festival celebrates the Coast's tradition of living off the land and sea, and the stallholders' passion for their wild ingredients is infectious. It is one of New Zealand's most distinctive food events and a genuine expression of West Coast culture. Check dates as the festival is held annually in early March.

reviews.summary: Foodies and adventurous eaters love the festival's unique offerings and party atmosphere. The whitebait is always a highlight. Book accommodation well in advance as Hokitika fills up quickly.

pois.name: Reefton Powerhouse Heritage
pois.short: Where New Zealand got its first electric street lights in 1888 — Reefton lit up before London and most of the British Empire.
pois.commentary: In 1888, the small West Coast gold mining town of Reefton became one of the first places in the Southern Hemisphere to have electric street lighting, powered by a small hydroelectric plant on the Inangahua River. The town was electrified before many parts of London and years ahead of most New Zealand cities. The restored powerhouse and heritage walk tell the story of this remarkable engineering achievement driven by gold mining wealth and frontier ingenuity. Interpretive panels along the walk explain the technology and the town's golden-age prosperity. Reefton has embraced its 'town of light' identity, and the heritage precinct is well-maintained. It is a fascinating example of how remote New Zealand communities sometimes led the world in innovation.

reviews.summary: History and engineering enthusiasts find the electrification story fascinating. The heritage walk is well-interpreted. Reefton itself is a charming small town worth exploring beyond the powerhouse.

pois.name: West Coast Wildlife Centre
pois.short: The South Island’s largest rowi kiwi incubation facility.
pois.commentary: The centre hatches eggs collected from the Ōkārito forest and raises chicks to a predator-safe size. Only about 750 rowi remain in the wild.

reviews.summary: Seeing the rowi kiwi hatchlings is special.

pois.name: Shantytown Heritage Park
pois.short: A recreated 1860s gold-mining town in the bush.
pois.commentary: Over 30 buildings, gold panning (everyone finds some), and a bush tramway behind a replica steam locomotive.

reviews.summary: A good family outing — kids love the gold panning and train ride.

pois.name: TranzAlpine Train
pois.short: One of the world’s great train journeys — crossing the Southern Alps from Christchurch to Greymouth.
pois.commentary: The TranzAlpine covers 223 km. Highlights include the Staircase viaduct, the Ōtira Tunnel (8.5 km), and the transition from Canterbury grasslands to West Coast bush.

reviews.summary: The scenery is breathtaking, especially through the Waimakariri Gorge.

pois.name: Hokitika Museum / West Coast Historical Museum
pois.short: The West Coast's largest museum and archive, housed in a Category II heritage Carnegie library building opened in 1908.
pois.commentary: The Hokitika Museum occupies the town's handsome 1908 Carnegie library — one of 18 Carnegie-funded libraries in New Zealand — which was declared earthquake-prone in 2016 and has since undergone seismic strengthening for reopening. The collections span the gold rush era of the 1860s, the pounamu trade, early European settlement, and the region's coal and timber industries. Archival photographs capture the raw, boom-town character of a Hokitika that once rivalled Dunedin for population. The museum holds artefacts, oral histories, and genealogical records central to West Coast family research. It remains the definitive cultural repository for the region.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently praise the depth and breadth of the collections at the Hokitika Museum — the gold-rush displays and pounamu exhibits are highlights. Many say the Carnegie building itself is worth the visit, and the staff are described as knowledgeable and welcoming.

pois.name: Sock World & Sock Knitting Machine Museum
pois.short: Hokitika's gloriously quirky museum of antique sock-knitting machines — where visitors can knit their own socks on 19th-century circular machines.
pois.commentary: At 93 Revell Street, Sock World houses one of the world's most comprehensive collections of antique circular sock-knitting machines — contraptions that look more like clockwork animals than domestic appliances. Most date from the late 19th and early 20th centuries, when mechanised sock production was genuinely cutting-edge. The machines are still operational, and visitors are invited to sit down and produce a sock to take home. It sounds eccentric, but it is one of the most memorable stops in Hokitika precisely because it is earnest and specific. The collection has been assembled over decades by enthusiasts and represents genuine industrial heritage from an era when a durable machine-knit sock was a household necessity.

reviews.summary: Sock World earns surprisingly devoted reviews for a sock museum — visitors describe it as charming, unexpected, and completely unique. The interactivity of knitting your own sock on a Victorian machine is what sets it apart. Recommended as a 45-minute detour for anyone who appreciates the genuinely odd.

pois.name: Arahura Marae / Ngāti Waewae
pois.short: Home marae of Ngāti Waewae, the hapū custodians of the Arahura River and its pounamu — the sacred greenstone source of the West Coast.
pois.commentary: Arahura Marae sits at the mouth of the Arahura River, just north of Hokitika — the very river whose pounamu deposits have been sacred to Ngāti Waewae and Ngāi Tahu for centuries. The wharenui Tūhuru opened in November 2014 after four years of construction, marking a significant cultural revival for the hapū. The name 'Arahura' refers to a path illuminated by mist, connecting the land to the spiritual world. Since 1997, all pounamu found in the Arahura catchment is legally vested in the Mawhera Incorporation (a Ngāi Tahu entity), restoring the iwi's authority as kaitiaki of this taonga. This is not a public visitor attraction — it is a living marae — but the surrounding landscape and river tell the story of one of New Zealand's most significant cultural relationships.

reviews.summary: Visitors who approach the Arahura area with respect and context describe it as profoundly moving — understanding that you are standing at the source of New Zealand's most sacred stone changes how you see the river and the mountains. Best experienced with a guided pounamu tour that includes cultural context from Ngāti Waewae.

pois.name: Denniston Plateau & Incline Heritage Walk
pois.short: Walk the historic Denniston Plateau — once home to NZ's largest coal-mining community, accessible only via the world-famous 1:1.3 gravity incline.
pois.commentary: Denniston, 18 km northeast of Westport at 518 m elevation, was once New Zealand's most productive coalfield and home to over 1,500 people at its peak in the early 1900s. The community was entirely dependent on the Denniston Incline — a 1,670 m rope-worked railway operating on a gradient of 1 in 1.3 — which lowered loaded coal wagons by gravity while hauling empties back up. The incline ran from 1880 to 1967. Today DOC manages the plateau and its surviving structures: the Brakehead building, engine foundations, brake drum emplacements, and remnant building platforms. The heritage walks reveal an archaeology of hardship and ingenuity. Views from the plateau over the Buller coalfields and out to sea are remarkable.

reviews.summary: Denniston rewards the drive up with extraordinary views and a haunting landscape of industrial archaeology. History enthusiasts rate it among the West Coast's best heritage experiences, and the walk through the surviving structures brings the incline era vividly to life. Go on a clear day for the views.

pois.name: Hokitika Gorge Scenic Reserve
pois.short: A 2 km return walk to a narrow gorge where the Hokitika River runs an impossible turquoise through sheer rock walls, crossed by a 90-metre suspension bridge.
pois.commentary: The Hokitika Gorge is 33 km inland from Hokitika, through dairy farmland that gives no hint of what waits. The Hokitika River is coloured that extraordinary turquoise by 'rock flour' — finely ground glacial sediment that scatters light in the blue-green spectrum. The walk follows the river through mature podocarp forest of miro, rimu, and kamahi before reaching the gorge itself, where the water forces between sheer rock walls. A 90-metre suspension bridge crosses the main channel above a flood braid, offering a view straight down into the gorge. The walk is graded easy and takes 40-60 minutes return. It is one of the most photographed spots on the West Coast for good reason — the colour of the water seems digitally enhanced even when you are standing in front of it.

reviews.summary: The turquoise water at Hokitika Gorge earns near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say they cannot believe the colour is real until they are standing beside it. The swing bridge is an added thrill, and the forest walk is beautiful in its own right. Described as unmissable on any West Coast itinerary.

pois.name: Ōkārito Lagoon & Kōtuku Wetland
pois.short: New Zealand's largest unmodified coastal wetland — home to kōtuku (white heron) and 70+ bird species, best explored by kayak from the tiny settlement of Ōkārito.
pois.commentary: Ōkārito Lagoon covers 3,240 hectares and is the largest unmodified coastal wetland in New Zealand. It is one of two places in the country where kōtuku (white heron) reside year-round — approximately 8–10 birds are present between October and March. The only breeding colony in New Zealand is at the Waitangiroto Nature Reserve immediately north. Over 70 bird species have been recorded, including 40 wading and migratory shorebird species. The village of Ōkārito itself is tiny — barely 30 permanent residents — and reached by a side road off SH6. Kayak rentals allow self-guided exploration of the lagoon's braided channels and wetland margins. The trig hill above the village offers views across the lagoon to the Southern Alps on clear days.

reviews.summary: Ōkārito is described by many visitors as the most beautiful and least-known place on the West Coast. Kayaking the lagoon at dawn, with the Southern Alps reflected in calm water and kōtuku wading in the shallows, is routinely described as a highlight of South Island travel. The remoteness and small scale are exactly the point.

pois.name: Gillespies Beach & Fur Seal Colony
pois.short: A remote black-sand beach south of Fox Glacier with a resident New Zealand fur seal colony and remnants of 1860s gold-mining tunnels.
pois.commentary: Gillespies Beach sits 21 km down a side road from Fox Glacier township, and the detour rewards with a landscape that feels genuinely remote. The beach is black-sand, backed by bush, with the Southern Alps visible inland. The fur seal colony is established at the southern end, accessible via a 30-minute walk through old gold-mining country that includes the remains of a miners' tunnel you can walk through. Beyond the tunnel, the track continues to Galway Beach and the main seal haul-out rocks. The seals are New Zealand fur seals (kekeno) — large, noisy, and entirely indifferent to human visitors at the appropriate distance. This combination of wildlife, coastal wilderness, and gold-rush archaeology makes Gillespies one of the most rewarding stops in Westland.

reviews.summary: Gillespies Beach earns consistently excellent reviews from visitors who make the detour — the seal colony is accessible and spectacular, and the miners' tunnel adds an unexpected historical dimension. Described as one of the West Coast's best-kept secrets by repeat visitors to the region.

pois.name: Cape Foulwind Walkway & Seal Colony
pois.short: A 3.4 km coastal walkway west of Westport that leads to one of the North West Coast's most accessible New Zealand fur seal colonies.
pois.commentary: Cape Foulwind extends into the Tasman Sea 11 km west of Westport, and the walkway from the lighthouse carpark to Tauranga Bay passes directly above a thriving fur seal colony on the rocks below. The seals — kekeno — are present year-round, but November to February is breeding season when pups are born and the colony is at its most active and vocal. The walkway traverses rocky headlands above dramatic coastal scenery, with views to the Southern Alps on clear days. Abel Tasman recorded sighting this headland in December 1642, making it one of the first European-documented landmarks on the South Island's west coast. The 3.4 km one-way walk (most people do the return) is graded easy with some steps.

reviews.summary: Cape Foulwind is rated as the best accessible seal colony on the northern West Coast — the elevated walkway puts you almost directly above the animals, and the ocean scenery is dramatic in its own right. Pairs well with a stop at Westport's Coaltown Museum. Allow 2 hours for the full round trip.

pois.name: Lake Kānihi / Lake Kaniere Scenic Reserve
pois.short: A 7,000-hectare glacial lake reserve 19 km southeast of Hokitika — deep, pristine, and surrounded by podocarp forest, with a historic gold-rush water race walk.
pois.commentary: Lake Kaniere is a glacial lake 8 km long and up to 195 m deep — one of the deepest in New Zealand — set within a 7,000-hectare scenic reserve. It lies between two mountain ranges and remains largely undeveloped, with clear water fed by mountain streams. Several short walks depart from the reserve margins, including the Canoe Cove walk through kahikatea swamp forest. The Kaniere Water Race Walk — part of the West Coast Wilderness Trail — follows the historic race dug in the 1870s to supply gold-sluicing operations with high-pressure water, a 3.5-hour route through mature podocarp forest. Picnic areas and a DOC campsite make this an excellent base for quiet exploration.

reviews.summary: Lake Kaniere rewards visitors seeking somewhere quiet and genuinely wild close to Hokitika. The water clarity is extraordinary, and the water race walk through the forest is a highlight. Less crowded than the Gorge, it is recommended for those who want more than a quick photo stop.

pois.name: Hokitika Pounamu Carving Studios
pois.short: Hokitika's cluster of working pounamu studios where you can watch Māori jade carvers at work and learn the cultural significance of New Zealand's most sacred stone.
pois.commentary: Hokitika is New Zealand's pounamu capital, and several studios on and near Revell Street offer genuine working carver experiences. Studios including Mountain Jade, Bonz 'n' Stonz, and Traditional Jade allow visitors to watch master carvers shape nephrite and bowenite into traditional and contemporary forms. The Arahura River — just north of town — has been the primary pounamu source for Ngāi Tahu for centuries; the stone's legal ownership was restored to the iwi in 1997. Pounamu holds a unique status in te ao Māori: it cannot be bought, only gifted or exchanged, and each form carries specific cultural meaning — hei tiki, hei matau, koru. Carvers explain these meanings and their own creative processes, making this a cultural experience as much as a shopping one.

reviews.summary: The pounamu studios of Hokitika are described by many visitors as the cultural highlight of the West Coast — seeing the stone being worked and understanding its meaning transforms it from a souvenir into something genuinely significant. The carvers' explanations of Māori significance are praised for being respectful and informative.

pois.name: West Coast Artist Workshop Trail
pois.short: A self-drive trail connecting working artists' studios across the West Coast — glassblowers, painters, photographers, and pounamu carvers.
pois.commentary: The West Coast's isolation has historically attracted artists drawn to its wild landscapes and independent culture. The Artist Workshop Trail links studios and galleries from Westport to Haast, including glassblowers, painters working in oils and watercolour, jewellers, photographers, and pounamu carvers. Unlike a retail experience, many studios allow visitors to see work in progress and discuss the artist's relationship with the landscape and light that defines their practice. The trail is self-directed — collect a map from any i-SITE — and the studios are concentrated enough around Hokitika that a half-day loop is feasible, while the full trail can structure an entire West Coast journey.
sources_to_check: West Coast NZ Artist Workshop Trail

reviews.summary: The Artist Trail is praised by visitors interested in more than scenery — the conversations with working artists in their studios are described as among the most memorable encounters of a West Coast visit. The glassblowers are a particular highlight for younger visitors.

pois.name: Okari to Lake Moeraki Fiordland Crested Penguin
pois.short: Remote rainforest coast south of the Haast Pass where Fiordland crested penguins — one of the world's rarest — nest in the coastal kahikatea forest during the breeding season.
pois.commentary: The coastline between Lake Moeraki and Monro Beach on the West Coast is the most accessible nesting site for Fiordland crested penguins (tawaki), a species endemic to New Zealand found only along the remote southwestern coastline and Stewart Island. During the August to November breeding season, penguins can be seen walking through the rainforest from the beach to their nest sites hidden in the understorey. The Monro Beach walk from the Lake Moeraki Wilderness Lodge passes through dense rimu, kahikatea, and kamahi forest to a remote sea-stack beach where penguins haul out at the base of the southern Alps. The West Coast wilderness setting and the extraordinary combination of penguin watching in a temperate rainforest make this unlike any other penguin experience in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: Wildlife enthusiasts and naturalists rate the Monro Beach tawaki walk as one of New Zealand's most extraordinary and remote wildlife experiences. Seasonal availability and the genuinely wild setting distinguish it from managed penguin attractions.

pois.name: Okarito Kiwi Sanctuary Night Tour
pois.short: The only guided kiwi tour in the South Island, searching for the rowi — New Zealand's rarest kiwi species — in the Okarito forest sanctuary at night.
pois.commentary: The Okarito kiwi sanctuary protects the world's entire population of rowi (Okarito brown kiwi), a species with fewer than 400 individuals remaining, all located within this small area of West Coast forest south of the Whataroa River. Ian Cooper of Okarito Kiwi Tours operates the only licensed guided kiwi night walk on the South Island, leading small groups through the dark podocarp forest in search of rowi, whose unique status as the rarest kiwi gives encounters an exceptional quality. The forest is also home to long-tailed bats, morepork, and glowworms visible along the route. The small village of Okarito is famous in New Zealand literature as the birthplace setting of Eleanor Catton's Booker Prize-winning novel The Luminaries.

reviews.summary: The Okarito kiwi tour is described by nearly all visitors as one of the most privileged and moving wildlife encounters in New Zealand, with hearing and then seeing wild rowi in their only remaining habitat being deeply affecting. Small group sizes ensure quality.

pois.name: Westland Petrel Colony Punakaiki
pois.short: The world's only colony of westland petrels near Punakaiki on the West Coast, where these large endemic seabirds return to nest in coastal hillside burrows after sunset.
pois.commentary: The westland petrel is one of the world's rarest seabirds, breeding only in a small area of steep coastal hillside near Punakaiki on the West Coast — nowhere else on earth. The colony of approximately 4000 pairs occupies burrows in forested hills above the sea and returns each evening from oceanic feeding flights, sometimes arriving in vast wheeling flocks at dusk that can be observed from the roadside or on guided tours. The birds mate for life and return to the same burrows each year from May to December. Punakaiki itself is famous for the Pancake Rocks and Blowholes, meaning visitors can combine the extraordinary geology of the coast with one of the world's most significant seabird encounters within a short distance.

reviews.summary: A truly specialist wildlife experience known primarily to keen ornithologists but deeply rewarding for any curious traveller. The sight of hundreds of large petrels returning at dusk to a coastal hillside is both dramatic and easily observable.

pois.name: Fox Glacier Valley Walk
pois.short: Helicopter-accessed ice walks on Fox Glacier, one of the Southern Alps' most accessible glaciers, exploring seracs, ice caves, and deep blue crevasses on guided tours.
pois.commentary: Fox Glacier is one of two famous glaciers on the West Coast (alongside Franz Josef, 23 kilometres north) that descend from the Southern Alps almost to sea level, a phenomenon unique in temperate latitudes worldwide. Heli-hike tours fly by helicopter from Fox Glacier village and land on the upper neve, where guides lead small groups through an ever-changing landscape of seracs, moulins, ice caves, and crevasses coloured a deep sapphire blue from compressed ancient ice. The helicopter approach also provides aerial views of the glacier's progress from the Main Divide to the terminal face and the surrounding peaks of Aoraki Mount Cook National Park. The glacier has retreated significantly in recent decades, making current experiences significantly different from those available twenty years ago.

reviews.summary: The heli-hike experience on Fox Glacier is frequently described as one of New Zealand's most extraordinary adventure activities, with the helicopter access, blue ice caves, and mountain scenery combining at a level difficult to match elsewhere.

pois.name: Monteith's Brewing Company / Greymouth
pois.short: Heritage brewery in Greymouth with tastings and behind-the-scenes tours.
pois.commentary: Monteith's has been brewing on the West Coast since 1868. The Greymouth brewery offers behind-the-scenes tours with tastings. The on-site restaurant and bar is one of Greymouth's best dining options. Tours run daily. The brewery sits on the banks of the Grey River in central Greymouth.

reviews.summary: Great brewery tour with generous tastings. The restaurant is excellent. Good shelter on a rainy West Coast day.

pois.name: West Coast Treetop Walk / Hokitika
pois.short: Elevated steel walkway through rainforest canopy with views to the Southern Alps and coast.
pois.commentary: The West Coast Treetop Walk at Lake Mahinapua rises 20 metres above the forest floor into the canopy of ancient West Coast rainforest. The walk culminates at a 47-metre-high viewing tower offering 360-degree panoramic views from the Tasman Sea to the Southern Alps. The walkway traverses rimu, kamahi, and miro trees hundreds of years old.

reviews.summary: Excellent canopy experience with stunning mountain views on clear days.

pois.name: Pancake Rocks & Blowholes / Punakaiki
