pois.short: Layered limestone formations and dramatic blowholes on the West Coast.
pois.commentary: The Pancake Rocks at Punakaiki are one of New Zealand's most recognisable natural landmarks — a series of limestone formations eroded into layers that resemble stacks of pancakes, perched on the rugged West Coast shoreline. At high tide and during heavy seas, powerful blowholes send plumes of seawater shooting skyward through gaps in the rock. A well-maintained loop track (15-20 minutes) winds through the formations with viewing platforms and interpretive panels explaining the 30-million-year geological process that created them. The blowholes are most spectacular in large swells at high tide — check tide tables for the best timing. The surrounding Paparoa National Park offers further walking options including the Pike29 Memorial Track. The cafe at the car park serves good food and coffee.

reviews.summary: The rock formations are extraordinary and the blowholes add drama. Time your visit with high tide for the best show.

pois.name: Jackson Bay / Okahu
pois.short: Remote end-of-the-road settlement with wild coastline and fresh crayfish.
pois.commentary: Jackson Bay is the end of the road on the West Coast — literally. Beyond this tiny fishing settlement there is nothing but wild forest and mountains for hundreds of kilometres. The bay was the site of a failed 1875 European settlement, and today only a handful of fishing families remain. The Cray Pot seafood shop at the wharf serves fresh crayfish, fish, and whitebait when in season. A short walk from the car park leads to Ocean Beach, a wild surf-battered stretch with views south toward the remote Cascade Valley. The Wharekai Walk loops through bush and wetland from the settlement. The drive from Haast through native bush is beautiful. Jackson Bay embodies the West Coast's wild, end-of-the-world character.

reviews.summary: The ultimate end-of-the-road destination. Fresh crayfish from the wharf is exceptional. Wild, remote, and atmospheric.

pois.name: Franz Josef Glacier Valley Walk
pois.short: Walk through rainforest to the face of one of the world's most accessible glaciers.
pois.commentary: The Franz Josef Glacier Valley Walk is a free, self-guided walk from the village through temperate rainforest to a viewpoint near the glacier terminal face. The walk (1.5 hours return) follows the Waiho River valley through a landscape dramatically shaped by glacial advance and retreat. Interpretive panels mark the glacier's former extent, showing how far it has retreated in recent decades — a visceral lesson in climate change. The glacier itself descends from the Southern Alps into rainforest at an elevation of just 300 metres — an almost unique occurrence globally. Heli-hiking tours offer the chance to walk on the glacier itself, landing via helicopter on the upper ice field.

reviews.summary: Awe-inspiring glacier accessible by an easy valley walk. The retreat markers are sobering. Heli-hike for the full experience.

pois.name: Shantytown Heritage Park
pois.short: Recreated 1860s gold-mining town with steam train, gold panning, and period buildings.
pois.commentary: Shantytown Heritage Park near Greymouth is a recreated 1860s gold-mining town that brings the West Coast's gold-rush history to life. The park features over 30 period buildings — a saloon, church, printing press, hospital, and miners' cottages — staffed by costumed guides. A working steam train loops through native bush. Gold panning in a sluice gives visitors the chance to try their luck (and keep any gold found). The park sits in bush that was itself part of the goldfields, adding authenticity to the setting. The steam train ride through the forest is a highlight, particularly for children. Shantytown provides context for the gold-mining heritage visible throughout the West Coast.

reviews.summary: Fun and educational gold-rush recreation. The steam train is great. Gold panning is addictive. Good family outing.

pois.name: Jackson Bay / Ōkahu — End of the Road
pois.short: The most remote settlement on the West Coast — a tiny fishing village at the literal end of the road.
pois.commentary: Jackson Bay (Ōkahu) is the most remote settlement on the West Coast — a tiny fishing village at the very end of the road south of Haast, where the pavement gives way to bush and ocean. The settlement has a wharf, a handful of houses, and the famous Cray Pot café serving fresh crayfish and fish from the local fleet. The Smoothwater Bay Track (30 minutes) leads to a wild beach with views of the Open Bay Islands. The Wharekai Te Kou Walk passes through dense coastal rainforest. The journey to Jackson Bay — through the Arawhata River valley with views to the glaciated peaks — is as much the attraction as the destination. This is genuinely the end of the line.

reviews.summary: The ultimate end-of-the-road experience. Fresh crayfish from the Cray Pot is legendary. Wild and remote. The journey is half the fun.

pois.name: Reefton — NZ's First Electric Town
pois.short: The first town in NZ (and one of the first in the world) with public electric street lighting.
pois.commentary: Reefton holds a remarkable distinction — in 1888, it became one of the first places in the Southern Hemisphere (and one of the first in the world) to have public electric street lighting, powered by a hydro-electric scheme on the Inangahua River. The Powerhouse Heritage site tells this story. The town's gold-mining heritage is visible in its Victorian architecture and the surrounding stamper batteries and mine sites. The Blacks Point Museum covers both the gold and electrical heritage. Reefton has recently attracted attention as a base for boutique outdoor experiences, with mountain biking trails and access to the remote Victoria Conservation Park. The town retains an authentic, unpolished West Coast character.

reviews.summary: Fascinating electrical heritage in a quirky gold-mining town. The Powerhouse site is interesting. Authentic West Coast character.

pois.name: Lake Matheson — Mirror Lake Walk
pois.short: Famous mirror lake reflecting Aoraki/Mt Cook and Mt Tasman in its dark, tannin-stained water.
pois.commentary: Lake Matheson is New Zealand's most famous reflective lake — its dark, tannin-stained waters create mirror-perfect reflections of Aoraki/Mt Cook and Mt Tasman in the Southern Alps. The reflections are best seen in early morning calm, before wind disturbs the surface. A well-formed loop track (1.5 hours) circles the lake through beautiful podocarp forest, with the View of Views platform at the far end providing the iconic reflection viewpoint. The lake formed when the Fox Glacier retreated approximately 14,000 years ago. The Reflection Café at the car park serves excellent coffee. Lake Matheson is one of New Zealand's most photographed locations, and on a calm morning, the reality matches the postcards.

reviews.summary: The iconic reflection view is extraordinary on calm mornings. Beautiful bush walk around the lake. Arrive early for the best reflections.

pois.name: Monteith's Brewing Company
pois.short: West Coast's iconic brewery with guided tours and tastings in Greymouth.
pois.commentary: Monteith's Brewing Company in Greymouth is one of New Zealand's most historic breweries, with brewing roots on the West Coast dating back to 1868. The brewery offers guided tours that take visitors through the brewing process, from ingredient selection to the finished product, with generous tastings of the full range including seasonal specials. The on-site restaurant serves hearty West Coast fare designed to pair with the beers. Monteith's is known for its range of craft-style beers including their popular Celtic Red, Original Ale, and seasonal Radler. The brewery tap room has a relaxed, welcoming atmosphere that reflects West Coast hospitality. A great rainy-day activity (and it does rain on the West Coast). Book tours in advance during peak season.

reviews.summary: Beer lovers enjoy the informative tours and generous tastings. The restaurant food is excellent. A great way to spend a West Coast afternoon, rain or shine.

pois.name: TranzAlpine Scenic Railway
pois.short: World-famous scenic train crossing the Southern Alps from coast to coast.
pois.commentary: The TranzAlpine is one of the world's great scenic railway journeys, traversing 223km from Christchurch across the Canterbury Plains, through the spectacular Waimakariri Gorge and Arthur's Pass, and down to Greymouth on the West Coast. The 4.5-hour journey crosses the Southern Alps, passing through 19 tunnels and over 4 viaducts, with the open-air viewing carriage providing unobstructed views of the dramatic alpine scenery. Highlights include the Staircase viaduct, the Otira tunnel (8.5km long), and the transition from golden tussock grasslands to lush West Coast rainforest. The train operates daily year-round, with morning departure from both ends. Booking essential. Many visitors ride one way and return by rental car or bus to see the scenery at different speeds.

reviews.summary: The alpine scenery is jaw-dropping, especially through the gorge sections. The open-air carriage is a highlight. A bucket-list railway experience. Book well ahead.

pois.name: Ship Creek Walks
pois.short: Twin short walks through ancient rainforest and along a wild driftwood beach.
pois.commentary: Ship Creek, between Haast and the Fox and Franz Josef glaciers, offers two short but spectacular walks that showcase the West Coast's untamed beauty. The Kahikatea Swamp Forest Walk (20 minutes) follows a boardwalk through an ancient kahikatea swamp forest — a prehistoric-looking landscape of towering trees rising from dark, mirror-still water. The Dune Lake Walk (30 minutes) crosses sand dunes covered in native vegetation to a wild, driftwood-strewn beach where the Tasman Sea meets dense rainforest. The combination of ancient forest, freshwater dunes, and wild coastline in such a short walk is unique in New Zealand. The walks are free, well-maintained, and suitable for all fitness levels. Sandflies can be fierce — bring repellent.

reviews.summary: The swamp forest is otherworldly and the wild beach is dramatic. Two very different walks in one stop. Bring insect repellent — the sandflies are legendary.

pois.name: Ross Gold Panning at Jones Creek
pois.short: Try your luck panning for gold in Jones Creek at Ross — where the largest gold nugget ever found in New Zealand was unearthed.
pois.commentary: Ross was one of the richest gold-mining towns on the West Coast, and in 1909 the 'Honourable Roddy' — a 99.5-ounce nugget, the largest ever found in New Zealand — was discovered here. Today you can pan for gold in Jones Creek using equipment hired from the Ross Information Centre. The technique is simple enough for children to enjoy, and there's still colour (fine gold) in the creek. The town itself is a time-capsule of gold-rush architecture, and the Water Race Walkway follows the original mining channel through regenerating bush. A hands-on history lesson.

reviews.summary: Great fun for families and history buffs. Most visitors find some fine gold. The heritage walk adds context. The town itself is charming and quiet. Allow time to explore.

pois.name: Welcome Flat Hot Pools (Copland Track)
pois.short: Natural hot pools in a glacial valley, reached by a 17 km tramp along the Copland Track — one of New Zealand's great backcountry rewards.
pois.commentary: Welcome Flat Hot Pools are natural geothermal pools nestled in the Copland Valley beneath the Southern Alps. Reaching them requires a 17 km, 6-7 hour tramp from SH6 near Fox Glacier — crossing swingbridges, river flats and dense rainforest. The DOC hut at Welcome Flat accommodates 31 and the hot pools sit just metres away, steaming against a backdrop of snow-capped peaks. Soaking in naturally heated water after a day's tramping, surrounded by mountains and forest, is one of the quintessential New Zealand backcountry experiences. Book the hut well ahead.

reviews.summary: Consistently rated one of New Zealand's best tramping experiences. The hot pools are magical. The walk is long but not technical. Book the hut months in advance. River crossings can be tricky after rain.

pois.name: Pororari River Track & Kayaking
pois.short: Kayak or walk through a stunning limestone gorge draped in native bush — a beautiful complement to the nearby Pancake Rocks.
pois.commentary: The Pororari River Track starts near Punakaiki and follows the river inland through a dramatic limestone gorge festooned with nikau palms, tree ferns and rata. The walking track is excellent — flat, well-maintained, and atmospheric — but kayaking the river adds another dimension entirely. Paddle upstream through the gorge, drifting beneath towering limestone bluffs and overhanging bush, then float back down. The combination of geological drama and lush vegetation is quintessentially West Coast. Kayak hire is available at Punakaiki and the river suits all abilities in normal conditions.

reviews.summary: The gorge scenery is stunning from both kayak and walking track. Very peaceful compared to busy Pancake Rocks nearby. Kayak rental is easy to arrange. Check river levels before paddling.

pois.name: Truman Beach & Track, Punakaiki
pois.short: A hidden beach near Punakaiki reached by a short bush track — wild, beautiful, and usually deserted.
pois.commentary: While crowds gather at the Pancake Rocks a few hundred metres away, Truman Beach sits in splendid isolation, reached by a short track through coastal bush and nikau forest. The beach is a raw sweep of West Coast sand backed by limestone formations and surf-battered rocks. Swimming is inadvisable due to the powerful surf and rips, but the walking, beachcombing and photography are superb. The track passes through beautiful nikau palm forest — one of the finest examples on the coast. It's extraordinary that a beach this beautiful is so consistently overlooked.

reviews.summary: A beautiful hidden beach just minutes from Pancake Rocks. The bush track is short and scenic. Not suitable for swimming. Often completely empty — a welcome contrast to the crowds.

pois.name: Formerly The Blackball Hilton
pois.short: A legendary West Coast pub in the coal-mining town of Blackball — birthplace of the New Zealand labour movement.
pois.commentary: Formerly The Blackball Hilton (renamed after the Hilton hotel chain took legal action) is a characterful pub-hotel in the former coal-mining settlement of Blackball. The town is famous as the birthplace of the New Zealand Federation of Labour, born out of the 1908 miners' strike. The pub serves hearty West Coast fare — enormous pies, salami made on-site, and cold beer — in an atmosphere thick with history and characters. The walls are covered in memorabilia from the mining era and the labour movement. It's a genuine piece of New Zealand social history that you can eat, drink and sleep in.

reviews.summary: A truly unique New Zealand experience. The salami is famous. The atmosphere is authentic and characterful. Accommodation is basic but full of charm. The drive to Blackball is scenic.

pois.name: Amethyst Hot Springs, Harihari
pois.short: Natural hot pools beside a cold mountain river between Franz Josef and Hokitika — a free, remote soak in the wild.
pois.commentary: The Amethyst Hot Springs are a series of natural hot pools on the bank of a crystal-clear river in the bush near Harihari. The pools are formed where geothermal water seeps up through river gravel, creating a patchwork of temperatures — you can mix hot and cold by shifting position or opening channels to the river. The setting is stunning: mountains rising on all sides, bush dripping with moisture, and the sound of the river. Access involves a short walk and a river crossing (check water levels). It's free, unmarked, and gloriously undeveloped. Ask locals for exact directions.

reviews.summary: A magical natural hot pool experience. The remoteness adds to the appeal. Not suitable after heavy rain when river levels rise. Finding it is part of the adventure — ask at your accommodation.

pois.name: Waiatoto River Safari (Jet Boat)
pois.short: A jet-boat safari up the remote Waiatoto River near Haast — deep into pristine rainforest and mountain wilderness.
pois.commentary: The Waiatoto River Safari takes you by jet boat into one of New Zealand's most remote river valleys, south of Haast. The river winds through virgin podocarp rainforest beneath the peaks of the Southern Alps, with no roads, no settlements, and no other visitors. Your pilot-guide shares stories of the valley's natural history, Māori pounamu trails, and pioneer settlements. The air quality is measurably among the purest on earth. Wildlife encounters — herons, shags, and sometimes kea — are common. It's one of the most genuinely wilderness experiences available on a day trip anywhere in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary wilderness experience. The guides are passionate and knowledgeable. The remoteness is awe-inspiring. Weather-dependent — be flexible with dates. One of the best day activities on the West Coast.

pois.name: Dolomite Point & Truman Track, Punakaiki
pois.short: A short bush track near Punakaiki to a dramatic coastal viewpoint over limestone cliffs and crashing Tasman surf.
pois.commentary: Dolomite Point and the Truman Track offer a complementary experience to the Pancake Rocks just down the road. The Truman Track drops through subtropical coastal bush — nikau palms, cabbage trees and tree ferns — to emerge at a dramatic coastal platform where limestone cliffs meet the Tasman Sea. On wild days the surf is spectacular, and on calm days the rock pools are fascinating. The walk takes only 15 minutes each way but the coast here is raw and beautiful. Combined with the Pororari River Track and Pancake Rocks, it forms a trio of walks that showcase the best of the Punakaiki coast.

reviews.summary: A short but rewarding walk to a dramatic coastal viewpoint. The bush is beautiful. Best combined with the Pancake Rocks visit. Watch for rough seas — stay back from the cliff edge.

pois.name: Ōpārara Basin Walks & Limestone Arches
pois.short: Ancient rainforest sheltering massive limestone arches and cave systems near Karamea — one of the West Coast's most dramatic natural wonders.
pois.commentary: The Ōpārara Basin hides one of New Zealand's most extraordinary natural landscapes deep in the Kahurangi National Park near Karamea. Walking tracks thread through primeval rainforest to reach the Ōpārara Arch — a massive limestone bridge spanning 43 metres over the river — and the smaller Moria Gate Arch. The forest is ancient and dripping, home to large carnivorous snails, powelliphanta, and a network of cave systems containing rare blind cave spiders and ancient invertebrates. The basin is reached by a 16 km gravel road from Karamea and feels genuinely remote. The arches are awe-inspiring in scale and the forest has a prehistoric atmosphere unlike anywhere else in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: A genuinely awe-inspiring natural wonder. The arches are massive and beautiful. The ancient forest is atmospheric. The access road is long and gravel but manageable. Worth the detour to Karamea.

pois.name: West Coast Wilderness Trail — Great Ride
pois.short: A 136 km Great Ride from Greymouth to Hokitika through rainforest, river valleys and gold-rush heritage — one of New Zealand's finest cycling trails.
pois.commentary: The West Coast Wilderness Trail is a 136 km Great Ride connecting Greymouth to Hokitika via Ross, threading through some of the most dramatic landscapes on the coast. The trail passes through ancient podocarp rainforest, across swingbridges over wild rivers, along old tramway routes and through gold-rush settlements. Most riders take 3-4 days, staying in small-town accommodation along the way. The trail surface is generally good but includes some technical sections through bush. E-bike hire is available and highly recommended for less experienced riders. The combination of rainforest, rivers, heritage and genuine West Coast remoteness makes this one of the country's most atmospheric cycling experiences.

reviews.summary: An outstanding multi-day cycling experience. The rainforest sections are stunning. Heritage adds depth. Some sections are technically challenging. E-bikes are recommended. Book accommodation ahead.

pois.name: Monteith's Brewing Co — Greymouth
pois.short: Historic West Coast brewery with tours and tastings.
pois.commentary: Monteith's has been brewing in Greymouth since 1868, making it one of New Zealand's longest-running breweries. The brewery tour takes you through the heritage brewing process and includes tastings of their full range, paired with stories of the West Coast's colourful brewing history.

reviews.summary: Great tour with generous tastings — steeped in West Coast character.

pois.name: Lake Kaniere Scenic Reserve
pois.short: Pristine bush-fringed lake with swimming, walks, and mountain reflections.
pois.commentary: Lake Kaniere is a large glacial lake surrounded by dense podocarp forest with mountain backdrops. The scenic reserve offers walking tracks through ancient rimu and kahikatea forest, and the lake's clean water is excellent for swimming — a peaceful alternative to the Hokitika Gorge crowds.

reviews.summary: Beautiful lake with pristine bush — much quieter than the gorge.

pois.name: Brunner Mine Historic Walk
pois.short: Heritage walk at the site of New Zealand's worst mining disaster.
pois.commentary: The Brunner Mine walk traces the site of New Zealand's worst mining disaster, where 65 men died in an 1896 explosion. The riverside trail passes mine ruins, the memorial, and interpretive panels telling the story of the West Coast's coal mining heritage with poignant detail.

reviews.summary: Sobering and important heritage site — the memorial is moving.

pois.name: Ross Historic Goldfields Walkway
pois.short: Heritage walkway through the town where New Zealand's largest gold nugget was found.
pois.commentary: Ross is where New Zealand's largest gold nugget — the 99-ounce 'Honourable Roddy' — was found in 1909. The heritage walkway passes water races, mine shafts, and the site of the discovery, telling the story of one of the West Coast's most productive goldfields.

reviews.summary: Interesting gold heritage walk — the nugget story is amazing.
